item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Title: Barber's Chair
Author: Danny Navarro
Release year: 2015

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide

Already has a good number of links, but could use a link to MCD so:

All identifying markings were forcibly removed from SCP-1738 prior to initial containment. The only identifying mark is a small logo reading "MC&D".

to
All identifying markings were forcibly removed from SCP-1738 prior to initial containment. The only identifying mark is a small logo reading "MC&D".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the oddly specific effects, I like the reason for it (though I agree it does not need to be spelt out), and I like how we don't know how it works. That said, I can think of absolutely no reason there wouldn't be tests. I don't see how it would burn through resources, given that presumably unless the phobia is triggered, the subject will not experience any negative effects. You could try using the keyboard to input the word 'styrofoam cup', for example, and then see what happens when you expose the cup to the subject. If nothing - you know it's more esoteric than that. That's a huge piece of information right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Foundation would at least start trying with simpler stuff. Problem is, the keyboard isn't really well explained, so we can't really tell how easy it'd be to figure out how to start with easy simple straightforward stuff like ladybugs and then escalate to specific things that we have lying around.

Sure, it'd be trial and error, but it doesn't mean the Foundation HAS to find what is the new phobia. "Undetermined" is not a completely unacceptable result, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, I really like some of the tests, and I like the note. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh, I dunno. The interesting part here is the super-specific fears noted in the addendum. But the note at the end is a little too meta (it provides a timeline to MC&D artifacts that coincides with their posting on the site), and keeps it from really feeling like an MC&D thing. I mean, I just sort of can't buy the explanation. Lastly, I feel like "programmable people" has been done before, but I could be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea about the GOI in the note at the end, but the article doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting. A crazy chair what makes people phobic of something, but it's completely at random. Well, random without the user manual.

The keyboard part maybe can be expanded upon. I see the value of having it, but at the same time, it's presently just kinda there. It's mentioned and forgotten. Just a couple of tests or something in which the Foundation tried using the keyboard on a D before testing was suspended might be useful for that.

Otherwise, I like it enough to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's "a massive expenditure" because we have to actually find out what they're suddenly terrified of. It takes no effort and little to time to shove a D into a machine and tinker with his fears. It might take a lot of effort and money to find a rare flower in the middle of a rainforest just to see if that's the terror. Or it could be something as simple as the color red, which takes no effort to find. It all depends on the difficulty to find the item in question. Just what it caught me as.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't see the point of the obscure keyboard. I mean, MC&D is a business, and they should be focused on accessibility and ease-of-use in order to have return customers. Having a crazy keyboard really doesn't make sense, and doesn't really add anything to the article IMO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhhhhh. I don't think this thing needs its raison d'etre spelled out. This is one of the few cases where a GOI identifier works to the article's advantage; why make a barber chair with weird shit in it that gives people new, obscure phobias? Well, it comes from an organization serving the hedonistic hyper-wealthy. Economical storytelling.

I'm obviously in the minority here, but the function that the note is trying to serve isn't needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hows it look now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could also get behind a more subtle note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like Kalinin, I found the note to the detriment of the article. It went from a sense of wonder and mystery to subtle as a brick to the face.I also agree that no note at all would be to the detriment of the article. Why not merely a more subtle note?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's funny, people in the forums told me it needed a note or else it'd be too boring. I'll see what others think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fought off the urge to downvote a GOI-affiliated article long enough to read it fairly, and I found myself liking the skip. The notion of the bored elite seeking new experiences through new sources of terror resonates, especially given the nature of this site.

That note at the end, though. Gaaaahhh. It removes any sense of mystery and imposes incredibly half-baked moralizing into something that doesn't need it (pro tip: nothing needs it).

Downvoting while the end note remains. I will upvote with enthusiasm if it's removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those that leave me on a "what" state. Almost as if the article itself doesn't quite understand what the item does and what are the implications of that.

I like the concept of an instrument capable of not only creating fear, but fabricating fear response. Is there a horrible implication to the fact that someone might be able to trick one's mind to be afraid of something on a level they can't possibly even understand? Oh yes, very much. Do I like the reason that MC&D give to invest on this machine? Also, yes, very much. I love that tone of cheap thrill-seeking.

But then I'm lost at several points. I'm lost as to how it would be a "massive expenditure of resources" to test this with D-Class, to the point that apparently no test was even conceived, so effectively we don't know how the effect can be controlled. I'm also lost at the recurrent madness mantra, but then that's one of those things I guess we should accept not to know fully. That, or perhaps I need to read more MC&D lore.

I like some of it, no votes for now. It has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been getting a bit of shit for editing without asking recently so just wondering if "three candles of candlelight" is what you meant to put.
Also I could just be being stupid but is there a little bit of tense-switching in the test logs?

+1 anyway this kept me interested the entire way through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: made some edits to address the issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
above the chart, you say
Subjects know to have been affected by SCP-1738

that should be
Subjects known to have been affected by SCP-1738

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely needs proofreading. I only had time to glance through it, and you were missing apostrophes, semicolons, and capital letters. Just a first impression.
